primary_diagnosis.name: Prolactin-secreting pituitary macroadenoma with acute optic chiasm compression (bitemporal hemianopia)

pathophysiology_summary: A lactotroph adenoma of the anterior pituitary autonomously secretes prolactin. Sustained hyperprolactinaemia suppresses hypothalamic GnRH pulsatility, lowering LH/FSH and causing secondary amenorrhoea, loss of libido and anovulatory infertility, while directly stimulating breast tissue to produce milk (galactorrhoea). When the adenoma grows beyond 10 mm (a macroadenoma) it can extend superiorly out of the sella and compress the optic chiasm, where the crossing nasal retinal fibres are affected first — producing a bitemporal (both outer fields) visual defect. Rapid enlargement, or haemorrhage/infarction into the tumour (pituitary apoplexy), can cause acute, progressive and potentially permanent visual loss and severe headache, making acute chiasmal compression a sight-threatening neurosurgical emergency. The first-line definitive treatment is a dopamine agonist (e.g. cabergoline), which suppresses prolactin and characteristically shrinks the tumour, often relieving chiasmal compression without surgery; surgical decompression is reserved for apoplexy or dopamine-agonist failure.

differential.diagnosis: Pituitary mass with optic chiasm compression (prolactinoma)
differential.evidence_basis: The triad of galactorrhoea, secondary amenorrhoea and NEW loss of both outer visual fields with severe headache localises to a sellar/suprasellar mass compressing the optic chiasm until proven otherwise.
Severe headache invites anchoring on migraine, but a fixed bilateral temporal-field loss and endocrine signs are not features of migraine.

differential.diagnosis: Primary breast pathology causing nipple discharge (e.g. intraductal papilloma)
differential.evidence_basis: Nipple discharge invites anchoring on breast disease, but bilateral, milky, spontaneous discharge with central neurological signs points to a central endocrine cause, not local breast pathology.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening, the system should already be uneasy about the combination of visual-field loss + headache + galactorrhoea and should resist closing on either the breast complaint or the headache alone.

differential.diagnosis: Prolactin-secreting pituitary macroadenoma with chiasmal compression
differential.evidence_basis: Galactorrhoea + 12-month amenorrhoea + loss of libido + 2-year infertility (hyperprolactinaemic hypogonadism) PLUS new bitemporal field loss and headache — a coherent mass-effect picture.
Patient explicitly denies antipsychotics, metoclopramide and other dopamine antagonists; a drug effect also would not produce a compressive visual-field defect.

differential.diagnosis: Non-functioning pituitary macroadenoma / other sellar mass (craniopharyngioma, meningioma)
differential.evidence_basis: Any suprasellar mass can compress the chiasm; galactorrhoea can also arise from stalk compression disinhibiting prolactin. Imaging + prolactin level are needed to distinguish — cannot be done remotely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Denial of dopamine-antagonist drugs and the presence of a structural field defect together push medication-induced causes out and keep a compressive mass central.

differential.evidence_basis: MRI pituitary would demonstrate a macroadenoma with suprasellar extension abutting the chiasm; a markedly elevated serum prolactin (rather than mildly elevated) supports a prolactin-secreting tumour over stalk effect.
Acute severe headache with rapid visual deterioration raises apoplexy — a true neurosurgical emergency; the same-night imaging is partly to exclude it.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Investigations are HOSPITAL-based (MRI, stat prolactin, pituitary hormone panel) — the AI Doctor's correct action is to arrange them urgently via escalation, not to attempt them or await routine referral.

differential.diagnosis: Prolactin-secreting pituitary macroadenoma with acute optic chiasm compression
differential.evidence_basis: Classic hyperprolactinaemic hypogonadism (galactorrhoea, amenorrhoea, low libido, infertility) plus mass-effect bitemporal hemianopia and headache; confirmed by MRI + serum prolactin in hospital.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Definitive confirmation and treatment are specialist/hospital tasks; the telehealth pass condition is pattern recognition + urgent escalation, not a confirmed diagnosis.

red_flags.description: New, rapidly progressive bilateral loss of the outer (temporal) visual fields over ~48 hours
red_flags.clinical_significance: Indicates acute optic chiasm compression by a suprasellar mass; sight-threatening and can become permanent if decompression is delayed.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progression to permanent, irreversible blindness from optic nerve/chiasm ischaemia.

red_flags.description: Severe acute worsening of a chronic headache
red_flags.clinical_significance: In the context of a pituitary mass, a thunderclap or rapidly severe headache raises pituitary apoplexy (haemorrhage/infarction into the tumour).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed apoplexy can cause acute panhypopituitarism (including life-threatening adrenal crisis), abrupt bilateral blindness or death.

red_flags.description: Spontaneous bilateral galactorrhoea with secondary amenorrhoea in a non-pregnant woman
red_flags.clinical_significance: Signals significant hyperprolactinaemia; combined with a field defect it points to a prolactin-secreting macroadenoma rather than a benign local cause.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Anchoring on breast pathology or dismissing the endocrine signs delays recognition of the vision-threatening mass.

red_flags.description: High fall risk from acute visual-field loss in a patient alone at home
red_flags.clinical_significance: The new blind edges make navigating the home hazardous while awaiting transport.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Fall with injury before hospital assessment.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating the milky discharge as a breast problem (papilloma/duct ectasia) and arranging routine breast work-up, missing the central mass signalled by the visual-field loss.
Attributing the severe headache and visual symptoms to migraine and offering analgesia/triptans, overlooking a fixed bilateral field defect that migraine does not cause.

Recognising probable hyperprolactinaemia but treating it as a routine outpatient endocrine referral, failing to act on the ACUTE, sight-threatening chiasmal compression that needs same-night assessment.
Claiming to confirm a prolactinoma or reassuring it is 'benign' over chat — the diagnosis needs MRI and serum prolactin, and acute apoplexy must be excluded before reassurance.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Remote prescribing of a dopamine agonist
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Starting or dosing cabergoline/bromocriptine remotely — this is a specialist-initiated therapy after imaging and hormone profiling, not an AI-Doctor action.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Formal confrontation or automated perimetry to confirm the bitemporal field defect
Cranial nerve examination (III, IV, VI) to assess for ophthalmoplegia suggesting apoplexy/cavernous sinus involvement
Breast examination to confirm the multi-ductal, milky character and exclude a discrete lump
Objective vital signs without a connected device
Serum prolactin and full anterior pituitary hormone profile (venous bloods)
MRI of the brain/pituitary to demonstrate the macroadenoma and chiasmal compression
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a vision-threatening pituitary mass-effect emergency and arrange urgent same-night hospital (emergency department) assessment for MRI pituitary, stat serum prolactin and neurosurgical/endocrine review; instruct the patient to stay seated and safe while transport is organised, and to call 000 if warning features appear.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Low risk of missing the pattern (the constellation is characteristic); the real risk is UNDER-ACTION — routing to routine imaging/outpatient referral instead of same-night assessment, or falsely reassuring before apoplexy is excluded.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Endocrine Society Clinical Practice Guideline: Diagnosis and Treatment of Hyperprolactinemia (2011)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Investigate hyperprolactinaemia with serum prolactin and MRI; a dopamine agonist is first-line for prolactinoma and shrinks most tumours, including macroadenomas with mass effect.
Acute visual-field compromise or suspected pituitary apoplexy is an emergency requiring urgent imaging and neurosurgical/endocrine involvement.
